q1.question: What is the default value of `flexDirection` in React Native?
q1.explanation: `flexDirection` defaults to `"column"` in React Native, so children stack vertically. This is the opposite of web CSS, which defaults to `"row"`. Switching to `"row"` makes children line up horizontally instead.

q2.question: What is the default value of `alignContent` in React Native?
q2.explanation: React Native defaults `alignContent` to `"flex-start"`, so wrapped lines align to the start of the cross axis. Web CSS defaults to `"stretch"`, which makes wrapped lines expand to fill the container — a common source of confusion when coming from the web.

q3.question: What is the default value of `position` in React Native?
q3.explanation: React Native defaults `position` to `"relative"`, so elements participate in the normal Flexbox flow. Web CSS defaults to `"static"`. Note that `"static"` does exist in React Native but only on the New Architecture, and it does not form a containing block for absolutely-positioned descendants.

q4.question: An absolutely-positioned element in React Native is offset relative to which ancestor?
q4.explanation: In React Native, an absolutely-positioned element is always offset relative to its nearest parent, regardless of that parent's own `position` value. In web CSS, the reference point is the nearest ancestor whose `position` is anything other than `"static"` — a key difference that trips up web developers.

options.2: Its nearest parent, regardless of that parent's `position` value

q5.question: Which statement about `useWindowDimensions` is correct?
q5.explanation: `useWindowDimensions` automatically re-renders the component whenever the window size changes — for example, on device rotation. No manual subscription is needed. `Dimensions.get()`, by contrast, returns a static snapshot and requires `Dimensions.addEventListener` to track changes.

options.1: It requires calling `Dimensions.addEventListener` to stay up to date when the device rotates
options.2: It re-renders the component automatically whenever the window size changes
options.3: It returns the full device screen size, including the status bar and navigation bar
options.4: It must be called inside `useEffect` to avoid reading stale dimension values

q6.question: On Android, what does `Dimensions.get("window")` exclude that `Dimensions.get("screen")` includes?
q6.explanation: `Dimensions.get("window")` returns the visible app area on Android, which excludes the status bar (unless it is translucent) and the bottom navigation bar. `Dimensions.get("screen")` returns the full device screen including those system UI areas. On iOS, both values are typically equal.

options.1: Nothing — both values are identical across all platforms
options.2: Only the status bar, but not the bottom navigation bar
options.3: The status bar and the bottom navigation bar (when not translucent)
options.4: The pixel density scale factor and font scale
